turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Give me the short version, what does Stitch do that a normal text to speech API doesn't?

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: A TTS API gives you a voice saying a line. Stitch renders the conversation around the line, who has the floor, when someone cuts in, how long the handoff breathes, and where the transcript should land.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Can I stop you there for a second?

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Yeah, go ahead.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That feels like the wedge, providers make voices, Stitch makes conversations.

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Exactly. If your app needs a coaching call, a sales roleplay, a tutoring session, or a customer interview, the hard part isn't only the voice, it's the timing between people.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So the input is still structured dialogue?

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Right, developers send speakers, turns, cues, and provider choices. Stitch returns mixed audio, transcript timing, a cost estimate, and the render metadata they need to ship it inside their product.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: But if the first render feels a little off, are they back to regenerating the whole thing?

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Not if they use Studio. Studio is the review bench. You load the render, listen against the transcript, tag the exact moment that feels slow, awkward, too quiet, or poorly delivered, then decide whether the fix is free mix work or paid targeted TTS.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So not edit the whole file every time something small feels off.

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Right, let me finish that part.

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: If one turn has a weird pause, Studio can regenerate only that stem and stitch it back into the existing mix. If the handoff is too slow, it can adjust the timing without calling the voice provider again. That matters when teams are testing a lot of variants.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's useful, especially if the developer is paying for every character.

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Exactly, and it keeps the quality loop honest, while the API gives you scale and Studio helps you find the recipe worth scaling.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Where does this start commercially?

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Developers building products where conversation is the interface, training apps, simulation tools, support QA, language learning, customer research, anything where the audio needs to sound reviewed, not pasted together.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Not podcasts first, that distinction matters.

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Right, not podcasts first.

turns.speaker: builder
turns.text: Podcasts are a possible output, but the business is developer infrastructure for conversation rendering.
